coredata.dc:title: Cellulosic nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots hydrogels with fluorescence/visco-elastic properties for pH- and temperature-sensitivity
coredata.dc:creator: Tohamy, Hebat-Allah S.
coredata.dc:description: The swelled hydroxyl ethyl cellulose-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propane sulfonic Acid) prepared from hydroxyl ethyl cellulose (HEC), nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N–CQDs) and poly(co-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propane sulfonic Acid) (poly(co-AMPS)), as base polymer, could be used as a pH/thermo-responsive hydrogels. The H3 hydrogels with 15 % N–CQDs in water undergo a reversible hydrophilic–hydrophobic phase transition around a LCST of 55 °C. Further, The HB, H1 and H2 exhibited the maximum swelling ratio at pH = 12, while, H3 exhibited the maximum swelling at PH = 6. This may be due to acidic groups of SA and/or the positively charge of N–CQDs. It was proved that HEC-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels are elastic in nature while the HEC-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels hydrogel is viscous in nature. Both viscoelastic moduli (G′ and G″) moduli modified over huge variety of angular frequency region. Further, the properties of HEC-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels grew to become in to elastic shape in comparison to HEC-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels without N–CQDs (i.e. HB). The H1, H2 and H3 (i.e. different forms of HEC-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels with different concentration of N–CQDs) may be applied in biomedical packages including building smooth and elastic tissues consisting of skin, skeletal muscle, and blood vessels, as well as, actuators for drug delivery use. But HB may be acclaimed as a scaffold for burned wound dressing. The fluorescence properties of the prepared hydrogels due to the fact of the addition of N–CQDs making it appropriate for use as a sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydroxy-ethyl cellulose
Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Swelling
Cellulosic hydrogels
Thermo-responsive
pH-responsive
Fluorescence
Visco-elastic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of (a) N–CQDs, (b) HB, (c) H1, (d) H2 and (e) H3.
Fluorescence spectra of N–CQDs, H1, H2 and H3.
SEM analysis of (a) HB, (b) H1, (c) H2 and (d) H3.
TGA/DTG of HB, H1, H2 and H3.
Mechanical properties of HB, H1, H2 and H3.
(a) swelling behavior, (b) Pseudo-first-order, (c) Pseudo-second-order, (d) Peppas and Peppas, (e) Fick's mechanism, (f) Peppas and Franson method, (g) PH behavior and (h) Thermal behavior.
Porosity (%), area (nm2) and pore size (nm) of HB, H1, H2 and H3.
Mechanical properties of HB, H1, H2 and H3.
Experimental swelling (We; exp. (g/g)) and models swelling percentages We 1; calc. (g/g); We 2; calc. (g/g) for HB, H1, H2 and H3.

head.ce:title: Cellulosic nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots hydrogels with fluorescence/visco-elastic properties for pH- and temperature-sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellulose and Paper Department, National Research Centre, 33 El Bohouth Str., P.O. 12622, Dokki Giza, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellulose and Paper Department, National Research Centre, 33 El Bohouth Str., P.O. 12622, Dokki Giza, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The swelled hydroxyl ethyl cellulose-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propane sulfonic Acid) prepared from hydroxyl ethyl cellulose (HEC), nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N–CQDs) and poly(co-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propane sulfonic Acid) (poly(co-AMPS)), as base polymer, could be used as a pH/thermo-responsive hydrogels. The H3 hydrogels with 15 % N–CQDs in water undergo a reversible hydrophilic–hydrophobic phase transition around a LCST of 55 °C. Further, The HB, H1 and H2 exhibited the maximum swelling ratio at pH = 12, while, H3 exhibited the maximum swelling at PH = 6. This may be due to acidic groups of SA and/or the positively charge of N–CQDs. It was proved that HEC-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels are elastic in nature while the HEC-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels hydrogel is viscous in nature. Both viscoelastic moduli (G′ and G″) moduli modified over huge variety of angular frequency region. Further, the properties of HEC-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels grew to become in to elastic shape in comparison to HEC-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels without N–CQDs (i.e. HB). The H1, H2 and H3 (i.e. different forms of HEC-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels with different concentration of N–CQDs) may be applied in biomedical packages including building smooth and elastic tissues consisting of skin, skeletal muscle, and blood vessels, as well as, actuators for drug delivery use. But HB may be acclaimed as a scaffold for burned wound dressing. The fluorescence properties of the prepared hydrogels due to the fact of the addition of N–CQDs making it appropriate for use as a sensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels are polymers that can absorb water without dissolving, making them water-swelling polymer networks [1,2]. The use of hydrogels in medicine has attracted a lot of interest especially that exhibit substantial variations in the degree of swelling in response to changes in the swelling medium conditions. The pH- variations in the gastrointestinal system are crucial. The majority of research has concentrated in particular on the effects of temperature or pH- because of the importance of these parameters in typical physiological, biological, and chemical systems [1]. Thermo-sensitive hydrogels have two states: enlargement and shrinkage. These gels expand when cooled below their lower critical solution temperature (LCST). The possibility of using pH- and thermo- sensitive hydrogels in controlled release systems has led to much research on these materials [3]. Gels are being thought of as promising materials for artificial organ, and actuators for drug delivery applications as a result of these properties [1]. The majority of hydrogels in use are made of natural or synthetic polymers, such as alginate, methacrylated gelatin, and gelatin hyaluronic acid. These traditional hydrogels cannot accurately simulate the biomechanics of soft and elastic tissues including skin, cartilage, skeletal muscle, and arteries since they are fragile and weak. For instance, the heart muscle is extremely elastic and may pump blood billion times over the period of a human life. So, Artery flexibility is essential. As a result, it would be advantageous to create high-strength, elastic, and biomaterial hydrogels that resemble such flexible and strong tissues. Many hydrogels with exceptional strength and elasticity have recently been created [4]. Due of their renewable, fully biodegradable, widely accessible, and highly biocompatible character, cellulose-based hydrogels have drawn the most interest among them. However, they have clear drawbacks that prevent them from being used to more widespread purposes. One such drawback is their poor mechanical strength [3,5]. In order to improve the mechanical properties and because of their high specific area, carbon polymers are typically added as a supplementary component to cellulose-based hydrogels to solve these issues [5]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are composed of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots, are carbon nanomaterials with a large surface area and oxygenated functional groups. GQDs are diamond-shaped, sp3 hybrid carbon-linked hybrid carbon nanoparticles with sp2 hybrid carbon and graphene oxide (GO) [6–8]. A variety of methods, including a hydrothermal and a pyrolytic carbonization, have been used to produce CQDs. Numerous studies have shown that using microwave heating is a feasible strategy for creating inexpensive CQD with a fast technique. Nitrogen doped CQDs (N–CQDs) are a kind of CQDs with nitrogen groups which could be interact with other polymers [6]. Additionally, there is an increase in demand for recycling of agricultural wastes. Recently, considerable effort has been put into using agricultural wastes. One of them was making carbon nanomaterials using mature beech pinewood sawdust (MB) and sugarcane bagasse (CB) as a feedstock [2,6,8–11].

As a result of the uniform dispersion of CQDs in the matrix and the strong electrostatic interaction between the –COOH of hydroxy ethyl cellulose (HEC) and the –NH2 functional group of N–CQDs, a HEC hydrogel with stable fluorescence characteristics was created. With great qualities like low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and low cost, the hydrogel created by crosslinking N–CQDs with HEC and 2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propane-sulfonic acid (AMPS) did not need specialized apparatus. The spectrum of applications for the hydroxyl ethyl cellulose- N–CQDs-g-poly(co-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propane sulfonic Acid) can be further expanded by investigating their use in the fields of fluorescence, PH, and thermo-sensitivity.

MB waste was obtained carpentry workshop was air dried. The 2-Acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propane-sulfonic acid (AMPS) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Both N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), and potassium persulfate (KPS; K2S2O8) were obtained from Sigma- Aldrich. Chemicals, reagents, and substrates employed in this study were of analytical grade and were used as received without any additional purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: 30 mg of MB with 70 mg of NaOH and 2400 mg of urea in 100 mL H2O were homogenized for 30 min. Then it was freezed in a refrigerator overnight to produce dissolved solution. After one night the defrosted mixture was ultrasonicated for 2 min. In a home microwave, the sonicated solution was heated at 700 W for around 7 min [6].

A homogenous solution was obtained after 2 g of HEC was dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water while being stirred at 50 °C. Potassium persulfate (0.24 g) diluted in distilled water (5 mL) was added when the temperature was raised to 60 °C. Then, an aqueous suspension of CQDs (2 g), AMPS (4 g), neutralised with an aqueous solution of NaOH to pH 5.5, and N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide (0.48 g) were added to the solution. The temperature was then kept at 65 °C for 10 min to produce free radicals on the HEC polymer chains. Finally, a pH of 5.5 and a temperature of 70 °C were maintained for 3 h. After that, distilled water was used to rinse away any extra water-soluble monomers, homopolymers, and crosslinkers from the produced hydrogel. The hydrogel was then kept at −80 °C for 3 h, after which it was freeze-dried. The attachment of AMPS to HEC and 0 %, 5 %, 10 %, and 15 % N–CQDs designated as HB, H1, H2, and H3, respectively [2,3].
Fourier-transform infrared spectra were collected employing Mattson 5000 spectrometer (Unicam, United Kingdom) using the KBr disk method.

The crystallinity was studied on X-ray powder diffraction as the diffraction patterns were measured by Bruker D-8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Germany) applying a40 kV voltage and a 40 mA current employing copper (Kα) radiation (1.5406 Å).
Cr . l
%
=
Sc St
X
100
where Sc = area of the crystalline domain, St = area of the total domain, ΔCrI (%) = change of crystallinity [2,6,9,10,12,13].

TGA analysis was performed on the prepared polymer powder employing Perkin Elmer thermogravimetric analyzer by heating the sample to 800 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. Kinetics of thermal decomposition was explored according to Coats-Redfern equations:
log
1 −
1 − ∝
1 − n
T 2
1 − ∝
= log
AR βE
−
E
2.303
RT
for
n ≠ 1
log
− log
1 − α
T 2
= log
AR βE
1 −
2 RT
E
−
E
2.303
RT
for
n = 1
where (E) is the activation energy [2,3,10,11].

All of the HEC-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels were created in glass tubes with a diameter of 4.61 mm and a length of 70 mm for the tensile testing as well as tensile loading unloading tests. At room temperature, with a cross-head speed of 0.25 mm/s, an ARES-G2 from TA Instruments (highest permissible force: 20 N) was used to evaluate the tensile characteristics of tiny, thin samples of the produced gels. We used samples with the following dimensions: diameter of 4.61 mm, total specimen length of 15 mm, and length between jaws of 3 mm. The evaluated hydrogel specimens were preserved by being wrapped in plastic after measurements to prevent water evaporation after the experiment with recovery of structure during sample resting.

Rheological properties of hydrogels prior to freeze-drying were measured on a strain-controlled ARES-G2 rheometer (TA Instruments, New Castle, DE, USA) using a parallel plate fixture with a diameter of 30 mm (plates with the cross-hatched surface to prevent slipping). Frequency sweep experiments were performed in a frequency range from 0.05 to 100 rad/s at strain amplitude of 1 % and a constant temperature of 25 °C. In addition, the linear viscoelasticity region was determined in view of the dependence of the storage modulus on the strain amplitude, which was measured at 25 °C at a frequency of 1 Hz.

The swelling behavior of the HEC-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels was observed by submerging 0.1 g of freeze-dried hydrogel samples in distilled water or buffer solution and incubating them at room temperature for 24 h. The bloated hydrogel was removed and weighed after being pushed to remove extra water between two filter sheets. The swelling equilibrium (sweq. %) was determined as follows:
sw eq
. % =
m t
−
m 0
m 0
100 %
where mt and m0 are the mass of the swollen gel at time t, and the mass of dry hydrogel, respectively [3,14].

Considering the first order kinetics:
ln

W
e 1

W t
=
K
1 t
While second-order kinetics:
t

=
1
K 2 We 2
+
1 We
t
We1 and We2 stand for the quantities of water absorbed by the gel at equilibrium for pseudo first and second-order models, respectively [3,15].

Peppas and Peppas described simple water-transport phenomena in hydrogel networks using the following equation (below 0.6, fractional swelling values):
ln
Wt We
= K
t n
A plot of ln
Wt We
versus ln t was used to calculate n and k, from the slope and intercept, respectively.

Based on Fick's law, a mutual diffusion coefficient (D) could be calculated:
ln
Wt We
=
4
π 0.5
Dt
L 2
n
The Peppas and Franson method was followed to estimate the water penetration velocity (υ) of the hydrogels:
υ =
dWt dWe
1 ρ
1
2 A
where Wt and We stand for the quantities of water absorbed by the gel in time ‘t’ and at equilibrium, respectively; k is the swelling constant, and is characteristic of the system under consideration and n represents the diffusion exponent that throws light on the mode of water transport into the gel [3,16,17].

The polymerization, aromatization, nucleation, and growth stages are all components of the N–CQDs production mechanism. Initially, intermolecular H-bonds are dehydrated and MB is denatured. A number of CC and CN bonds develop inside the MB as a result of intra-molecular dehydration brought on by microwave heating, creating aromatic clusters that serve as a precursor to N–CQDs. A burst in the nucleation of N–CQDs will occur when the concentration of aromatic clusters approaches the critical supersaturation point [18].

The FTIR spectra of the prepared N–CQDs revealed absorption bands at 3431.06 (NH), 3337.10 (OH), 2939.03 (CH), 1674.75 (amide I), 1591.86 (amide II), 1437.77 (C=C), 1213.89 (O–C=O), 1151.79 (C–O–C) and 1045.39 (CN) cm−1. The presence of NH and CN vibrations proves the preparation of N–CQDs [6]. The FTIR spectrum of the HEC-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels differed from N–CQDs as evidenced by the increased relative absorbance (RA) of the characteristic absorbance peaks of OH (i.e. 0.10, 0.35, 0.76, 1.28 and 1.71) and CO (i.e. 0.06, 0.34, 0.75, 1.25 and 1.68) for N–CQDs, HB, H1, H2 and H3, respectively. The oxygenated groups are high in H3 compared to the other gels due to the high content of N–CQDs. The OH of HB shifted from 3326.65 cm−1 to 3315.08, 3303.58 and 3295.80 cm−1 for H1, H2, and H3, respectively. This shift proved the stronger intermolecular H-bonding between OH groups in the grafting reaction with –NH of AMPS and NH2 groups of N–CQDs [2,3,6,9,10]. A new peak around 1110–1153 cm−1 for HB, H1, H2 and H3 appeared due to asymmetric vibration of SO (Fig. 1 ) [2,3].

N–CQDs, H1, H2 and H3 were excited at 350 nm and showed the maximum emission wavelength at 429.0, 430.0, 438.0 and 432.5 nm, respectively. Fluorescence emission is caused by the oxygen vacancy defects of the N–CQDs' surfaces. The emission peaks at 507.0, 492.5, 495.5 and 498.0 nm are derived from the C = O/C = N moieties of N–CQDs in N–CQDs, H1, H2 and H3, respectively. This difference in the position of the peaks is attributed to the variation in the crosslinking between HEC and AMPS with N–CQDs [6]. The H3 hydrogel has high fluorescence intensity due to its high N–CQDs content (Fig. 2 ).

From TEM investigation, it was discovered that N–CQDs contain embedded graphene oxide (GO) sheets and a ball-like shape. The N–CQDs observed with a size range between 7.23 and 10.31 nm. Evidence that GO is made up of a few layers of sheets that stack together can be found in the color gradient on the sheet's edges (Fig. 3 ) [6,10].
At 800 °C, the mass loss (ML%) for HB, H1, H2, and H3 was found to be 59.67, 69.65, 74.32, and 66.78 %, respectively.

Two decomposition stages were visible in HB's degradation curve. The moisture content is responsible for the 1st weight loss between 52.8 and 131.86 °C, with a maximum temperature of 46.11 °C and an average ML of 8.10 %. The 2nd ML stage is divided into two stages, with maximum temperatures of 222.21 and 342.15 °C and an average ML of 46.44 and 5.13 %, respectively. These stages are located between 146.62 and 259.39 and 263.42–726.95 °C. This ML stage is attributed to carbonization and degradation of the carbonized residues [2,3].

Three decomposition stages were visible for the decomposition curves of H1, H2, and H3. The 1st weight loss occurred between 32.53 and 131.66, 36.69–143.36 and 32.27–148.81 °C, with maximum temperature of 55.69, 67.94 and 83.28 °C and average ML values of 5.07, 5.62 and 7.42 % for H1, H2 and H3, respectively. With a maximum temperature of 299.74, 306.76 and 307.61 °C and an average ML of 49.78, 47.34 and 37.99 %, the 2nd ML stage is located between 162.25 and 354.27, 212.12–364.30, and 179.20–389.84 °C for H1, H2 and H3, respectively. Pyrolytic degradation is attributed to this ML stage. The 3rd ML stage is in between 359.72 and 743.34, 465.29–735.97 and 357.08–744.78 °C, with a maximum temperature of 397.10, 601.97 and 404.29 °C and an average ML of 14.38, 21.36 and 21.37 % for H1, H2 and H3, respectively. In this stage, the remaining carbonaceous material is broken down to produce low-molecular-mass volatile compounds [2,6,10–12].

According to the previous findings, the HB degrades in two stages, making it less stable compared to H1, H2, and H3. The H3 has greater thermal stability than H1 and H2 because to the higher total ML% and maximum temperature of the 2nd main stage. More ordered, H-bonded, and densely packed N–CQDs may be the cause of H3's strong thermal stability, which may also raise H3's thermal decomposition temperature (Fig. 5 ) [3,6,10,11].

After grafting with N–CQDs, the rheological behavior of HB is drastically altered. The highest shear modulus (G) is found in HB. It suggests that H1, H2 and H3 are a lot more elastic than HB. This indicates that the elasticity improved when the N–CQDs were added. Simultaneously, the storage modulus (G′) for H3, which is 0.49 Pa and 512.50 % larger than that of HB, rises with increasing N–CQDs content. The distinctive increase in G′ of H1, H2, and H3 over HB may be explained by the formation of a more bonded network created by the addition of N–CQDs with nitrogenized and originated free functional groups, meaning that elastic property is more prominent than viscous property, as well as by the formation of homogenous hydrogels with free functional groups [3,12]. As can be observed from the figure, over the whole angular frequency range (0.1–100 rad/s), the G′ of water swollen hydrogels is higher than the loss modulus (G″) These moduli don't vary as much on frequency (ω), which is a feature of cross-linked hydrogels in general [19].

The values of G′ and G″ start rising. But it is seen that the increase of G′ is greater than the increase of G″. As rises, the hydrogel's elastic characteristics also rise. Additionally, it is envisaged that when more load is placed on H3, G′ will exceed G″. As a result, the interactions between cross-linking and bonding inside the polymer become strong, favoring elastic rather than viscous properties. In contrast, the contacts inside HB become weaker and more dispersed, which results in the gel's transition from an elastic to a viscous state. At lower ω region, the values of tan (δ) are almost steady and then gradually decrease and then start increasing with ω. The tan δ values of H3 are low over the whole range of ω, which means that elastic property is more predominant than viscous property [3,12,19].

The actual applications of hydrogels are significantly influenced by their mechanical properties. The stress-strain curves of the HB, H1, H2, and H3 hydrogels under compression are shown in Fig. 6c. The usual “J” shape curves of HEC-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) were all present, demonstrating their strong compressive strength. The compressive strength of HEC-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels increased when N–CQD content rose (from H1 to H3). The outcome showed that H3 hydrogels with higher N–CQD content had networks that were dense and strong, which increased compressive strength. About 568.81 Pa was the maximal mechanical strength of H3, exceeding the strength of pure HB (106.40 Pa). Compared to the pure HB hydrogel, the H3 hydrogel had a lower strain. The outcomes showed that the addition of N–CQDs significantly improved the mechanical properties of the hydrogels made up of HEC, N–CQDs, and poly(co-AMPS). N–CQDs thus significantly contributed to the increase in mechanical strength of these samples. This can be attributed to the formation of more physical cross-linking points inside the hydrogel and improved its network topology in H1, H2 and H3 compared to HB. Further, the concentration of elasticity (CEAC) of H3 was the highest which proves our previous explanations (Table 2 ).

The swelling ratio initially increases first rapidly and then slowly, attaining maximum continuous swelling. This is a noteworthy characteristic that is clearly seen in the curves of the swelling ratio vs time. The swelling equilibrium (sweq.%) of the H, H1, H2, and H3 gels was measured at 14 h with a pH of 7.0 to be 771.96, 1372.18, 1862.43, and 1627.54 %, respectively. The AMPS is a highly hydrophilic monomer with anionic character due to the presence of sulfonic acid (SA). A natural polymer is HEC. Due to the increasing N–CQD content in the hydrogel, the values of sweq.% of the H2 gels rose. The swelling of H2 is bigger than the swelling of HB and H1 because the hydrophilicity of N–CQDs becomes greater than that of AMPS. Due to the numerous highly charged groups of N–CQDs, the structure of the H2 has strong electrostatic interactions. The positively charged groups produced by nitrogen hetero-atoms in N–CQDs will increase the electrostatic double-layer attraction to negatively charged SA groups [3,14,20]. With a rise in N–CQD content, the swelling ratio of hydroxyl ethyl cellulose-CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels rose, indicating N–CQDs' higher hydrophilicity than HEC. Further, due to the high saturation of N–CQDs, which enhanced the positively charged surface charge and created repulsion, the sweq.% value for H3 decreased [3,21].

Pseudo-first-order kinetic proved successful in predicting a more accurate response for swelling in all the H, H1, H2, and H3 when comparing the two kinetic models. It was discovered that K1 provides the best We1 value and coefficient of correlation (R2) for HB. In other words, HB showed physisorption mechanisms. K2 provides the best R2 and We2 value for H1 and H2 at the same time. Therefore, chemisorption processes were seen in H1 and H2. The R2 is provided by K2, and We1 is close to We;exp. The result is that H3 displayed both physisorption and chemisorption processes [3,6,22]. For HB, H1, H2 and H3; n = 0.39, 0.28, 0.46, 0.32; indicates Fickian diffusion [3,23]. The D value of H2 improved with the high N–CQDs content in the networks (i.e. 0.04) due to its higher swelling capacity and started to decreased with high saturation of N–CQDs for H3 (i.e. 0.02) [3,16]. The υ of H2 has the highest value, indicating its higher swelling capacity compared to HB, H1 and H3 (Table 3 ).

Fig. 7 displays the HB, H1, H2, and H3 responses to pH from pH 3 to pH 12. At pH 12, the HB, H1 and H2 showed the highest swelling ratio, whereas H3 showed the highest swelling at pH 6. Because of the acidic SA groups and/or the positively charged N–CQDs, the volume of HB, H1, H2, and H3 changed over a wide pH range. The shift in sweq.% is caused by various pH-dependent interaction species in the swelling media. Therefore, in acidic and basic media, respectively, the involved species are primarily –COOH and SO3H from AMPS (acid form) and –COO−, SO3 −2 and K+ from AMPS (basic form), as well as the positive charge from N–CQDs [3,23–25].

At pH 3 and 6, the acid form (–COOH and SO3H) could create intermolecular hydrogen bonds that limit the anion-anion electrostatic repulsion, which in turn causes ion pair formation to lower the osmotic pressure inside the hydrogel. Because the polyelectrolyte impact was ignored, the network tended to shrink and collapse, which decreased sweq.% for HB, H1, H2 and H3 [3,23–25]. In addition the AMPS converted into ionised carbonate (–COO−, SO3 −2, and K+) at pH 9 and pH 12 as well as N–CQDs will be positively charged, resulting in stronger hydrophilicity and higher electrostatic repulsion of the network, which will increase the capacity for HB, H1, H2, and H3 to absorb water. However, in the initial conditions for H3, more Na+ and positively charged N–CQDs would shield the repulsion of the negatively charged –COO− and SO3 −2 groups, causing the formation of chemical cross-linking and charge screening effect (causing the shrinking of H3 which is highly saturated with N–CQDs), and subsequently its swelling ratio decreased [3,23,24]. The swelling differences of the HB, H1, H2, and H3 gels are mostly caused by the ionic groups. These findings imply that altering the solution's pH can influence the swelling behavior of HB, H1, H2, and H3.

The thermal responsiveness of the HB, H1, H2, and H3 caused a continual increase in the swelling ratio with changing reaction parameters when the temperatures were varied from 35 to 75 °C (Fig. 7 h). The overall pattern suggested that as temperature rises, HB swelling diminishes. The introduced hydrophilic monomers (i.e. N–CQDs) promotes the formation of H-bonds with thermo-sensitive monomers and increases the LCST. Above the LCST, the H-bonds break, and the H1 and H2 dehydrate, releasing water molecules. The swelling of H1 and H2 begins to rise at 75 °C. This might result from a rise in thermal mobility of polymer molecules inside the H1 and H2 [3,26]. For H3, increasing the temperature to 55 °C causes the swelling to rise. This could be as a result of the polymer molecules inside the H3 being more thermally mobile. The swelling starts to go down between 65 and 75 °C for H3. This might be because more positively charged N–CQDs would shield SO3 −2 groups from the repelling effects of the negative –COO−, creating a chemical cross-linking and charge screening effect that caused H3, which is highly saturated with N–CQDs, to shrink and, as a result, its swelling ratio to decrease [3,23,24]. Compressive strength and the swelling ratio of H3 are known to be compatible. When N–CQDs were added to the HEC-N–CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogel, the cross-linking density increased, resulting in a stronger network structure. H3 exhibited a traditional thermo-senitive profile, as seen in Fig. 7 h. The swelling ratio of H3 decreased when the ambient temperature rose from 65 to 75 °C. Intermolecular H-bonds are formed below the LCST (i.e. 55 °C) and intra-molecular H-bonds are formed above the LCST for H3. The conflict between intra- and inter-molecular hydrogen bonding interactions in the H3 system was thought to be the cause of H3's thermo-sensitivity. The samples' noticeable swelling changes with temperature further confirmed the H3's thermo- sensitivity. Our swelling capacity results are higher than results obtained from El Sayed et al. in which the sweq.% was 150,140, 230, and 70 % at 30, 40, 60, and 80 °C, respectively [27].

The liquid microwave method was used to prepare N–CQDs from MW by eco-friendly, low cost and simple technique. The prepared N–CQDs were used to achieve the hydroxyl ethyl cellulose-CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogels. The morphological image confirms the existence of porous structure within the hydroxyl ethyl cellulose-CQDs-g-poly(co-AMPS) hydrogel matrix. It was observed that the porosity, surface area and thermal properties were enhanced with increasing the N–CQDs content. Further, it was proved that H1, H2 and H3 are elastic in nature while HB is viscous in nature. In water, the H3 hydrogels with 15 % N–CQDs undergo a reversible hydrophilic–hydrophobic phase transition around a lower critical solution temperature (LCST) of 55 °C, below which H3 hydrogel is in highly water swollen state. With increase in the temperature above LCST, the hydrogel collapses and expels most of the water molecules. Further, The HB, H1 and H2 exhibited the maximum swelling ratio at pH = 12, while, H3 exhibited the maximum swelling at PH = 6. The volume of HB, H1, H2 and H3 changed in a wide range of pH value due to acidic groups of SA and/or the positively charge of N–CQDs. The different pH-depended interacting species in swelling medium lead to the change of sweq. %. Therefore, the involving species are mainly –COOH and SO3H from AMPS (acid form) at acidic medium and −COO−, SO3 −2 and K+ from AMPS (basic form) as well as the positive charge from N–CQDs at basic medium. Additionally, the effect of the N–CQDs on the dynamic viscoelastic properties, after penetrating inside the hydrogel matrix has been evaluated. Both moduli showed significant change over wide range of angular frequency region and the nature of HB turned from elastic to viscous. In contrast, the interactions of cross linking and bonding inside the H3 get strong which means that elastic property is more predominant than viscous property. Based on the observed basic properties, HB can be recommended as a scaffold for burned wound dressing, while, H3 might be of interest in constructing soft and elastic tissues such as skin, skeletal muscle, and blood vessels, as well as, actuators for drug delivery use.
The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author acknowledges the National Research Centre and the Academy of Scientific Research and Technology (ASRT), Egypt and Czech Academy of Sciences, for financial support of the bilateral research activities.
